title: Long-Run Risk: Is It There?
abstract: This paper documents the existence of a persistent component in consumption growth. We take a novel approach using news coverage to capture investor concern about economic growth prospects. We provide evidence that consumption growth is highly predictable over long horizons—our measure explains between 23% and 38% of cumulative future consumption growth at the five-year horizon and beyond. Furthermore, we show a strong connection between this predictability and asset prices. Innovations to our measure price 51 standard portfolios in the cross section and our one-factor model outperforms many benchmark macro- and return-based multifactor models.

article.introduction:
The difficulties the canonical consumption-based asset pricing model faces in matching key empirical moments in both the time series (Campbell and Shiller (1988), Fama and French (1988)) and the cross section (Hansen and Singleton (1982,1983)) have sparked a large theoretical literature.1While many models have been developed to account for these empirical regularities, a common critique of these models is that their assumptions are difficult to verify in the data. Moreover, by construction, many of their predictions are similar, making them hard to distinguish empirically. One of the leading channels is based on the assumption of a persistent component in consumption growth, known as “long-run risk.” If such a component exists, small shocks to this component can be amplified through certain preferences, such as Epstein and Zin (1989) preferences, to generate large fluctuations in asset prices.2In this paper, we show that consumption growth does indeed contain a persistent component and that this component has significant cross-sectional asset pricing power.
To formalize our empirical tests, we start by deriving predictions common to models featuring a persistent component of consumption growth. First, the level of the persistent component should predict future consumption growth, especially at long horizons. Second, innovations to this component appear in the stochastic discount factor and therefore should be priced in the cross section of asset returns.
To test these predictions, we construct a measure that captures the persistent component of consumption growth using media coverage. This choice is motivated by the idea that media sources capture investor concern about economic growth. Specifically, news coverage about the economy should increase when investors are concerned about poor economic growth prospects.3Accordingly, we proxy for investor concern about economic growth prospects using articles in theWall Street Journal(WSJ), the main source of significant marketwide news. We construct our measure by parsing articles in the WSJ for words related to economic growth and then aggregating to the monthly level. This procedure results in a monthly measure of investor concern about economic growth from 1927 to 2013. We refer to this news measure as the “N-index.” Because high (low) concern about growth prospects should correspond to lower (higher) expected consumption growth,4we normalize the N-index and flip the sign so that higher (lower) values of the N-index correspond to higher (lower) conditional expected consumption growth. To address potential measurement error in the N-index, we apply a one-sided Hodrick-Prescott (HP) filter to the N-index and extract the low-frequency trend component. We refer to this filtered measure as the “HN-index” and use both measures in our tests.
We begin by testing the prediction that our measure of the persistent component predicts future consumption growth. We show that the N-index positively predicts future consumption growth up to 96 months (eight years) out, with the predictive power increasing over the horizon. The predictive power of the N-index peaks at the 72-month (six-year) horizon with an adjustedR2of around 25%. These results are robust to corrections for overlapping samples and persistent regressors. We verify the out-of-sample predictive power of the N-index using the procedure proposed in Goyal and Welch (2007). In addition, we show that our predictability results are robust to the inclusion of other known predictors of short-term consumption growth, namely, real labor income growth, dividend-price ratio, and realized industrial production variance.
The consumption predictability results are even stronger using the HN-index, the low-frequency component of the N-index cleaned of measurement noise. The HN-index explains 37% of the variation in future consumption growth at the six-year horizon compared to the adjustedR2of 25% in the baseline N-index specification. These results are also robust to corrections for overlapping samples and persistent regressors, and the predictive power remains strong in the Goyal and Welch (2007) out-of-sample tests.
To address measurement concerns in the consumption data, we run predictive regressions using several alternative measures of consumption growth, including electricity utilization growth from Da, Huang, and Yun (2016), Q4 consumption growth from Jagannathan and Wang (2007), garbage consumption growth from Savov (2011), unfiltered consumption growth from Kroencke (2017), unfiltered consumption growth Q4-to-Q4 from Kroencke (2017), and CO2 emission growth from Chen et al. (2020). These alternative measures of consumption growth are all highly predictable at long horizons by both the N- and the HN-indices. For example, the N-index (HN-index) explains 44% (62%) of the variation in cumulative future electricity utilization growth at the eight-year horizon. Further tests using data from Malloy, Moskowitz, and Vissing-Jorgensen (2009) show that long-horizon stockholder consumption growth responds more than nonstockholder consumption growth to the N-index.
Next, we test our second main prediction, that innovations to the persistent component of consumption growth enter into the pricing kernel and price assets in the cross section. We construct the innovations to our news measure by running an AR(1) process on the N-index and extracting the residuals, which we refer to as the “NI-index.” In this formulation, we would expect the loadings on the NI-index to command a positive risk premium. We follow the same procedure to measure innovations to the HN-index, which we refer to as the “HNI-index.” We test the cross-sectional asset pricing power of the NI-index (HNI-index) by implementing the Fama and MacBeth (1973) two-pass regression approach on a set of 51 standard test portfolios. As predicted, loadings on the NI-index (HNI-index) are compensated by positive and significant risk premiums. The NI-index outperforms the Fama and French (1993) three-factor model and performs comparably with the Fama and French (2016) five-factor model in pricing the 51 test portfolios. The HNI-index outperforms the Fama and French (1993) three-factor model as well. The significance of the NI-index (HNI-index) is robust to the inclusion of the Fama-French factors.
The CAPM and Fama-French factor models provide a useful benchmark for evaluating the asset pricing power of the NI-index and the HNI-index models. However, a comparison with economically motivated macroeconomic factors may provide additional economic insight. We compare the NI-index and the HNI-index with the risk factors from Parker and Julliard (2005), who construct their factors as the future consumption growth over various horizons, termed “ultimate consumption risk factors.” Their measure is intuitively related to our factors since the N-index (HN-index) predicts consumption growth at long horizons and the NI-index (HNI-index) is constructed as innovations to this measure. We find that the ultimate consumption risk factors provide additional asset pricing power beyond the NI- and the HNI-indices, but the coefficient estimates on the NI- and the HNI-indices remain positive and are mostly significant. Our results are also robust to the inclusion of the historical consumption growth factors from Dittmar and Lundblad (2017) as well as a comprehensive set of macroeconomic variables similar to those in Ludvigson and Ng (2007,2009).
As a further test of our asset pricing results, we generate entirely new portfolios based on the beta exposures of individual assets to the NI-index (HNI-index). We form portfolios from the universe of NYSE, AMEX, and NASDAQ stocks by sorting on each firm's preranking betas on the NI-index (HNI-index) and find that the long-short strategy based on the preranking betas generates statistically significant average excess returns of about 3% per year. Furthermore, portfolio returns, Sharpe ratios, and ex post betas are largely monotonic in portfolios based on the preranking betas.
While the consumption predictability results indicate that the N-index (HN-index) captures information about a persistent component in consumption growth, discussion about economic prospects could also be driven by changes in stochastic volatility or by time-varying uncertainty. We study these channels by testing the extent to which our predictability and asset pricing results are driven by second-moment information embedded in our measures. We construct a measure of stochastic volatility as the trailing realized variance of the NI-index. We remove the volatility component from the N-index by regressing it on the trailing realized variance and extracting the residuals. We confirm that the residuals predict future consumption growth at long horizons. We follow the same procedure to clean the HN-index of second-moment information and show that its consumption predictability power remains strong at long horizons. Next, we construct innovations to volatility as the residuals of our stochastic volatility measure fitted to an AR(1) model and include these innovations as a pricing factor alongside the NI-index. We find that the pricing power of the NI-index is not driven by innovations to stochastic volatility. We run a similar test using the HNI-index and find that the adjustedR2of the HNI-index model improves with the inclusion of these innovations to stochastic volatility, but the magnitude of the coefficient estimate on the HNI-index is similar to that in the stand-alone model. In addition, we include common measures of macroeconomic uncertainty as pricing factors alongside the NI-index (HNI-index) in our cross-sectional regressions. The macroeconomic uncertainty measures include realized industrial production variance, economic political uncertainty from Baker et al. (2016), the geopolitical risk measure from Caldara and Iacoviello (2022), the realized daily stock market variance, and the news-implied volatility (NVIX) index from Manela and Moreira (2016). The coefficient estimates on the NI-index remain significant at the 1% level with the inclusion of these second-moment measures.5Taken together, the results suggest that our measure primarily captures information about a persistent component of consumption growth rather than stochastic volatility or uncertainty.
Our work relates to a large body of literature that seeks to connect macroeconomic fundamentals to asset prices. Early work in this literature includes Hansen and Singleton (1982,1983) and Chen et al. (1986). More recently, Lettau and Ludvigson (2001a,2001b) demonstrate that thecaymeasure is able to predict aggregate stock market returns and price assets in the cross section. However, several subsequent papers (e.g., Lewellen, Nagel, and Shanken (2010), Nagel and Singleton (2011), Daniel and Titman (2012)) show that their conditional CCAPM (and many others like it) does not explain the cross section of returns. Savov (2011) shows that an alternative consumption measure—garbage—is able to better price assets in the cross section than the canonical consumption expenditure measure. Bansal, Kiku, and Yaron (2012), Bansal et al. (2014), and Schorfheide, Song, and Yaron (2018) offer support for the existence of a persistent component of consumption growth using statistical methods. Barro (2006) provides evidence in support of the rare disaster channel. Cremers, Fleckenstein, and Gandhi (2021) show that the Treasury yield-implied volatility can predict macroeconomic activities such as gross domestic product and consumption. Most closely related to our paper, recent studies show that long-run aggregate consumption growth can better capture the cross-sectional stock returns (e.g., Parker and Julliard (2005), Hansen et al. (2008), Malloy, Moskowitz, and Vissing-Jorgensen (2009)). In particular, Parker and Julliard (2005) show that cumulative future consumption growth prices the Fama-French 25 size-BM portfolios in the cross section. We contribute to this literature by employing a new approach to provide direct evidence in support of the existence of a persistent component of consumption growth.
Our research is also related to the growing textual analysis literature. Tetlock (2007) shows that the tone of news predicts stock market movements. Li (2008) finds that the readability of 10-K filings is related to firm earnings. Using textual analysis of 10-K filings, Hoberg and Phillips (2010,2016) construct a text-based industry categorization. In addition, a number of papers use textual analysis to capture higher-moment information of the macroeconomics. For example, Baker et al. (2016) construct a policy uncertainty measure based on word count from newspapers, while Caldara and Iacoviello (2022) develop a measure of geopolitical risk that they use to predict real activity and stock market returns. Fisher, Martineau, and Sheng (2022) capture investor attention using newspaper coverage. Manela and Moreira (2016) use a machine-learning technique to extract information about the VIX from newspaper text and extend the VIX measure back to 1890. In this paper, we show that news contains important information about the dynamics of the consumption growth process. In particular, we extract information regarding the persistent component of consumption growth embedded in the news.
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. In SectionI, we derive the general framework motivating our empirical tests. SectionIIdescribes the data collection process and measure construction. SectionsIIIandIVdocument our main empirical results. SectionVexamines how second-moment information affects our results, and SectionVItests the robustness of our results. SectionVIIconcludes.
article.I. Model:
Equation (8) provides the basis for our asset pricing tests. The expected excess return of an asset depends on its exposure to innovations to both the long-term and the contemporaneous components of consumption growth. The model implies that information that captures shocks to the persistent component of consumption growth should price assets in the cross section. In the following tests, we focus on the persistent component, since contemporaneous consumption growth has been well documented to have poor asset pricing power.
