article.II. Data:
We construct our time-series news measure, the N-index, by identifying WSJ articles containing the following words: “Consumption,” “GDP,” “Gross Domestic Product,” “GNP,” “Gross National Product,” or “Pig Iron.” Our objective is to choose words whose inclusion in an article best indicates that the article reflects investor concern about economic growth. We aim to keep our word choice as simple and direct as possible to limit the degrees of freedom. We include “Pig Iron” as a proxy for consumption in the early part of the measure because the national accounting system was not developed until the mid-20thcentury.7We hand check a random sample of articles comprising the news measure and confirm that the majority of these articles focus on economic growth. The coverage is typically elevated when concern about economic growth is high, consistent with both academic studies (Robinson (2007), Trussler and Soroka (2014)) and anecdotal evidence, which suggests that news tends to focus on negative events since they are more attention grabbing. We expect that times of high coverage around economic growth are periods when investors are concerned about poor economic growth prospects.
We obtain our news data from two primary sources: Factiva and ProQuest. Factiva provides full-text front-page articles of the WSJ from 1994 to 2013. We count the occurrence of the keywords in these front-page articles each month to obtain the monthly news measure. We focus on front-page articles as these reflect the most important topics of the period. To extend the measure further back, we combine Factiva data with WSJ articles from Proquest. Proquest contains WSJ articles from 1927 to 1999 but it is difficult to obtain full-text articles from its database. Furthermore, Proquest does not differentiate front-page articles from others, so we count the number of articles that contain any of the keywords each month to construct the early portion of the news measure. To combine the two parts of the news measure, we demean each series and scale the Factiva data to have the same standard deviation as the ProQuest series. We combine the series by matching the first month of the Factiva sample (January 1994) with the Proquest sample. Our results are robust to different pasting choices, which we discuss below. We detrend the entire news series and normalize the measure to have a standard deviation of one. For intuition, we flip the sign so that higher (lower) values of the N-index correspond to higher (lower) conditional expected consumption growth. Panel A of Figure1shows the time series of the news measure from January 1929 to December 2013. The measure is depressed in the early part of the sample during the Great Depression and at the end of the sample near the Great Recession. From 1945 to 1999, the news measure is relatively elevated, coinciding with the period of stable growth.
We build a number of alternative news measures based on different pasting and word choices to test the robustness of the results. First, we construct a version of the N-index using a different pasting choice to combine the Proquest series with the Factiva series. We construct the measure by scaling the Factiva series by the ratio of the two standard deviations (Proquest to Factiva) in the overlapping period as the scaling factor, and refer to this measure as the alternative-pasting N-index. Next, we decompose the N-index by separating consumption from the output-related words, we construct a consumption-only N-index using only the word “consumption,” and an output-related N-index using the output-related words “GDP,” “GNP,” “Gross National Product,” “Gross Domestic Product,” and “Pig Iron.” In additional robustness checks, we construct a measure using “Industrial Production” and a separate measure using “Payroll Employment” as a keyword. We also construct a version of our measure that includes “Industrial Production,” a version that includes “Payroll Employment,” and a final measure that includes both words alongside the baseline phrases. We discuss the performance of these alternative choices in SectionVI.
We use a number of macroeconomic time-series data in our study, including real consumption growth (Δc$\Delta c$), electricity utilization growth (Δe$\Delta e$), dividend-price ratio (dp$dp$), real labor income growth (Δl$\Delta l$), realized industrial production variance (rv$rv$), the Baker and Wurgler (2006) sentiment measure (bw$bw$), the ultimate consumption risk factors from Parker and Julliard (2005), and thecaymeasure from Lettau and Ludvigson (2001b). To construct the real consumption series, we sum nominal Personal Consumption Nondurable Goods and nominal Personal Consumption Services and deflate by the Consumer Price Index: Total All Items for the United States. We obtain real consumption growth by taking differences of the logged real consumption level. The authors of Da, Huang, and Yun (2016) kindly provide us with their electricity utilization growth rate series. Dividend-price ratio data come from Robert Shiller's personal website. We construct the real labor income growth series by taking differences in logs of real per-capita disposable personal income from the Bureau of Economic Analysis. We construct realized industrial production variance following Bansal et al. (2014) by taking the sum of the squared past 12 months of industrial production growth rates from the Federal Reserve Economic Data. We obtain the Baker and Wurgler (2006) residual sentiment measure from Jeffrey Wurgler's personal website. We construct the Parker and Julliard (2005) ultimate consumption risk factors from the consumption growth data. The consumption-to-wealth ratio, orcay, comes from Sydney Ludvigson's website. Furthermore, we construct and obtain several alternative consumption growth measures: we construct the garbage consumption growth measure following Savov (2011), we obtain unfiltered consumption growth, Q4 consumption growth, and unfiltered consumption Q4-to-Q4 from Tim Kroencke's personal website, and we construct CO2 emission growth following Chen et al. (2020).
Panel A of TableIpresents the summary statistics for the news measures and a number of the macroeconomic variables. Variables in logged differences are multiplied by 100 for ease of interpretation. Panel B of TableIshows the correlations between the news measures and the macroeconomic variables. The N-index (HN-index) exhibits low contemporaneous correlation with all macroeconomic series except the dividend-price ratio. The NI-index (HNI-index) is not strongly correlated with any of the macroeconomic variables.
In the asset pricing tests, we use 51 test portfolios: 25 Size-BM, 10 Momentum, 10 Industry, and six Treasury bond portfolios sorted by maturity. These portfolios provide a stringent test of factor models. The choice of the Size-BM, Momentum, and Treasury Bond portfolios follows Adrian et al. (2014). We also include 10 Industry portfolios following the suggestion of Lewellen, Nagel, and Shanken (2010). Data for the 45 equity portfolios come from Kenneth French's website and data for the six bond portfolios are collected from CRSP.
We use a number of measures of risk and uncertainty in our asset pricing tests, including the Economic Policy Uncertainty (EPU) measure of Baker et al. (2016), the Geopolitical Risk Index (GPR) of Caldara and Iacoviello (2022), realized stock market volatility (RMV), and the NVIX from Manela and Moreira (2016).
We compare the N-index, NI-index, HN-index, HNI-index, and several alternative versions of these measures with contemporaneous macroeconomic and financial variables of interest. We present the results in TableII. We find that the indexes generally load positively on the market return, suggesting that positive returns are associated with long-term higher expected consumption growth. Moreover, the indexes tend to load negatively on the realized stock market variance and EPU measures—increases in stock market variance and political uncertainty are associated with decreases in the persistent component of consumption growth. We include month indicator variables (omitting January) and report the coefficients. There does not appear to be a strong seasonal pattern in the indexes.
article.III. Consumption Growth Predictability:
There are well-documented issues with predictive regressions when the regressors are persistent. We run our results through a battery of stringent robustness tests including alternative standard errors, thet/T$t/\sqrt {T}$test suggested by Valkanov (2003), small-sample inference bootstrapping tests, and the out-of-sample predictability test proposed by Goyal and Welch (2007). Moreover, it is well known that monthly consumption growth data suffer from potentially serious measurement errors (see Slesnick (1998)). To address this concern, we test the predictive power of the news measure using several alternative consumption measures, including electricity utilization growth from Da, Huang, and Yun (2016), Q4 consumption growth from Jagannathan and Wang (2007), garbage consumption growth from Savov (2011), unfiltered consumption growth from Kroencke (2017), unfiltered consumption growth Q4-to-Q4 from Kroencke (2017), and CO2 emission growth from Chen et al. (2020). In addition, we regress stockholder and nonstockholder consumption growth from Malloy, Moskowitz, and Vissing-Jorgensen (2009) on the N-index to investigate the differential sensitivity of different segments of the market to the persistent component.
Following equation (14), we regress cumulative future real consumption growth fromt+1$t+1$tot+K$t+K$on the N-index at timetfor a range of values ofK$K$.8The N-index specification in TableIIIdocuments the predictive regression results. The coefficients are uniformly positive and are significant at the 5% level for all monthly horizons until approximately 96 months (eight years) out. The adjustedR2s of the predictive regression increase over the horizon until 72 months (six years) out, reaching approximately 25%. Our findings document a strong predictive component of consumption growth. Panels A and B of FigureIA.1in theInternet Appendixshow the monthly coefficients and adjustedR2s across all horizons up to 120 months (10 years).9Previous research shows that consumption growth may be predictable at the short horizon by real labor income growth, dividend-price ratio, and realized industrial production variance. We run multivariate predictive regressions controlling for these three variables. The coefficients on the N-index remain positive and significant at the same horizons. The point estimates do not change materially from the univariate specification—the adjustedR2s of the multivariate regressions peak at the six-year horizon at 28%.
Next, we test the predictive power of the HN-index to forecast consumption growth. The HN-index captures the low-frequency component of the N-index cleaned of potential measurement errors. We present the results in TableIIIin the HN-index specification. Removing measurement noise improves the predictive power. The coefficients on the HN-index are positive at all horizons and significant at the 5% level up to eight years. The adjustedR2s from the HN-index predictive regressions are uniformly higher than those using the N-index across all horizons. The HN-index explains 38% of the variation of cumulative future consumption growth at the five-year horizon compared to the N-index's adjustedR2of 23%. Panels C and D in FigureIA.1show the monthly coefficients and adjustedR2s across all horizons up to 120 months (10 years). The predictive power of the HN-index is robust to the inclusion of real labor income growth, dividend-price ratio, and realized industrial production variance. The coefficients on the HN-index remain positive at all horizons and significant at the 5% level at horizons up to six years with the inclusion of the other predictor variables. TableIA.Ishows similar results after controlling for thecay.
A number of well-documented econometric issues can arise in long-horizon predictive regressions using persistent regressors. The first results from the use of overlapping samples in predictive regressions. Valkanov (2003) shows that long-horizon regressions with an overlapping sample and persistent regressors can lead to serious inference problems. In particular, thet-statistics diverge over long horizons and theR2statistics converge to one. The second type of inference problem is caused by the use of persistent regressors when the shocks to the dependent and independent variables are correlated (see Stambaugh (1999)).
Our first test addresses the Valkanov (2003) critique. Valkanov (2003) shows that the commont-statistics in the time-series long-horizon predictive regressions may not converge to well-defined distributions. This issue is particularly severe when the overlapping sample comprises a significant portion of the data and when the regressor is highly persistent. Valkanov (2003) suggests using a scaledt-statistic, or thet/T$t/\sqrt {T}$statistic. We implement thet/T$t/\sqrt {T}$test and simulate the critical values following Valkanov (2003) using parameters calibrated to our data. The results are reported in TableIA.II. The N-index specification reportst/T$t/\sqrt {T}$statistics as well as Newey-West and Hodrick adjusted standard errors. The Newey-West, Hodrick, andt/T$t/\sqrt {T}$test statistics are all significant at the 5% level before the 96-month (eight year) horizon.
The HN-index specification of the table also presentst/T$t/\sqrt {T}$statistics and Newey-West and Hodrick adjusted standard errors for the HN-index predictability regressions. The HN-index coefficients are all significant at the 5% level up to the 96-month (eight year) horizon using Newey-West and Hodrick standard errors. Thet/T$t/\sqrt {T}$statistics indicate significance at the 5% level at all horizons up to 72 months (six years).
The second test addresses the critique raised by Stambaugh (1999). Stambaugh (1999) shows that the presence of correlated shocks to the dependent and independent variables will bias coefficient estimates in predictive regressions when the regressors are persistent. We estimate the Stambaugh (1999) bias and find that the economic magnitude of the bias is small—the upper bound of the bias is about 2% of the coefficient estimate. Furthermore, we conduct small-sample inference using bootstrapping methods. We bootstrap (with replacement) our data under the null hypothesis of no consumption growth predictability and calculate the confidence intervals of thet-statistics and the adjustedR2s over all horizons. We document the results in TableIA.III. In the N-index and HN-index consumption predictability regressions, both thet-statistics and the adjustedR2s fall outside the 95thpercentile of the bootstrapped distributions for most specifications. For the N-index predictability regressions, the N-indext-statistics fall outside the 95% confidence interval for the one- to six-year horizons. The adjustedR2s fall outside the 95% confidence interval for all horizons. For the HN-index predictability regressions, the HN-indext-statistics fall outside the 95% confidence interval for the one- to five-year horizons and the adjustedR2s fall outside the 95% confidence interval for the one- to six-year horizons.
In-sample predictability does not imply that individuals can take advantage of this predictability in real time. Goyal and Welch (2007) propose an alternative test of the predictive power of explanatory variables. The idea is to compare the out-of-sample explanatory power of the predictive variables against that of the simple historical mean of the dependent variable. We calculate the equivalent statistics for the consumption growth predictability regressions. The out-of-sampleR2s inform us whether economic agents, econometricians, and policymakers can use the N-index in real time to forecast future cumulative consumption growth better than using forecasts based on simple historical average consumption growth. We document the results in TableIA.IV. The N-index specifications show the in-sample and out-of-sampleR2s at horizons from 12 to 120 months out. For the univariate predictive regressions, the out-of-sampleR2s are positive at every horizon, peaking at 26% at the six-year horizon. The multivariate N-index specification shows the predictive regression results for the multivariate regressions, including the N-index, real labor income growth, dividend-price ratio, and realized industrial production variance. In this specification, the out-of-sampleR2s are similarly high, peaking at 30% at the six-year horizon. Similar to the N-index results, for the univariate predictive regressions, the HN-index out-of-sampleR2s are positive at every horizon, peaking at 39% at the five-year horizon. The multivariate HN-index specification shows the predictive regression results for the multivariate regressions, including the HN-index, real labor income growth, dividend-price ratio, and realized industrial production variance. In this specification, the out-of-sampleR2s are similarly high, peaking at 41% at the five-year horizon. The results from the out-of-sample tests confirm our in-sample findings that consumption growth is strongly predictable by the N- and the HN-indices across the one- to eight-year horizons.
Prior literature highlights the potential shortcomings of the monthly consumption data series due to measurement errors.10To address concerns that monthly consumption data may be unreliable, we test the predictive power of the N-index (HN-index) using several alternative consumption measures.
Da, Huang, and Yun (2016) show that electricity consumption serves as a good proxy for consumption while being at higher (monthly) frequencies. We run predictive regressions using electricity consumption growth and present the results in TableIA.VIn the N-index specifications, we show the N-index coefficients and Newey-West adjusted standard errors from the time-series predictive regressions at different horizons. The N-index positively predicts electricity consumption growth, consistent with our findings for consumption growth. The coefficients are highly significant at all horizons and the adjustedR2peaks at 44% at the 96-month (eight-year) horizon. The predictability results are robust to the inclusion of real labor income growth, dividend-price ratio, and realized industrial production variance. For the HN-index specifications, the coefficient estimates are positive and significant at the 5% level at every horizon. The HN-index explains 62% of the variation in electricity utilization growth at the six- and eight-year horizons. The predictability results are robust to the inclusion of real labor income growth, dividend-price ratio, and realized industrial production variance. In the multivariate specification, the coefficients on the HN-index remain positive and significant at the 5% level at all horizons.
We test the predictive power of the N-index (HN-index) using several alternative consumption growth measures available at the annual frequency: Q4 consumption from Jagannathan and Wang (2007), garbage consumption from Savov (2011), unfiltered consumption from Kroencke (2017), unfiltered consumption Q4-to-Q4 from Kroencke (2017), and CO2 emission growth from Chen et al. (2020). We run predictive regressions at annual horizons up to 10 years and present the results in TableIA.VI. The data frequency is annual since these alternative consumption measures are available only at this frequency.
The results based on the alternative consumption growth measures are consistent with our baseline consumption growth predictability and electricity utilization growth predictability results. The N- and the HN-indices strongly predict these alternative measures of consumption growth across horizons up to 10 years. The coefficients are uniformly positive for all consumption growth measures at all horizons, and the adjustedR2s are high at around 30% to 40% at the medium and long horizons. For example, the second panel of TableIA.VIpresents the predictive regression results using the garbage consumption growth measure from Savov (2011). The left-hand side documents the results using the N-index and the right-hand side documents the results using the HN-index. The coefficients on the N-index are positive and significant at the 5% level at all horizons. The coefficients on the HN-index are positive at all horizons and significant at the 5% level at all horizons except the 10-year horizon. The N-index explains 37% of the variation in cumulative garbage consumption growth at the five-year horizon compared to the HN-index's adjustedR2of 35%. The coefficients on the N- and the HN-indices remain positive at all horizons and significant at the 5% level at the two-year horizon and beyond with the inclusion of the other predictor variables. Results based on the other alternative consumption growth measures are qualitatively similar.
Malloy, Moskowitz, and Vissing-Jorgensen (2009) provide measures of consumption growth for stockholders and nonstockholders based on the Consumer Expenditure Survey. We test the predictive power of the N-index using the consumption growth data series provided on their website. The data extend from 1982 to 2004 at the monthly frequency, which is about half of the length of the consumption growth data from our main test. The smaller sample size reduces the power of the test, but we would expect the coefficient on the N-index to remain positive. Moreover, these data allow us to compare the predictive power of the N-index for the consumption growth of stockholders versus nonstockholders. We find that the N-index predicts the consumption growth of both groups with a positive sign over all horizons and document these results in TableIA.VII. The coefficient estimates on the N-index are larger in magnitude for stockholders relative to nonstockholders. The coefficients on the N-index for nonstockholders are more significant than those for stockholders. However, this result may be attributable to differences in measurement error between the two series. The stockholder consumption growth data are calculated using about one-fourth of the observations per month compared to the nonstockholder data.11
article.IV. Cross-Sectional Asset Pricing:
Many papers restrict the set of test assets in the Fama and MacBeth (1973) two-pass regression to the 25 Size-BM portfolios. Lewellen, Nagel, and Shanken (2010) highlight a problem with this approach by demonstrating that these portfolio returns can be approximately spanned by the three Fama and MacBeth (1973) factors. This makes the 25 test portfolios a low hurdle for an asset pricing model to overcome. To address this concern, we test the asset pricing power of the NI-index using 51 portfolios: 25 Size-BM, 10 Momentum, 10 Industry, and six Treasury Bond portfolios. This provides a stringent test of the asset pricing power of the NI-index. We compare the performance of the NI-index against that of the CAPM, Fama and French (1993) three-factor model, and the Fama and French (2016) five-factor model. Finally, we test the pricing power of the NI-index alongside a broad set of macroeconomic variables including the ultimate consumption risk factors from Parker and Julliard (2005), the historical consumption growth factors from Dittmar and Lundblad (2017), as well as a comprehensive list of variables similar to Ludvigson and Ng (2007,2009).
TableIVdocuments the second-stage regression results (equation (17)) and compares the pricing power of the NI- and the HNI-indices against that of the CAPM, the Fama and French (1993) three-factor model, and the Fama and French (2016) five-factor model. The table reports results based on specifications with and without intercepts. We discuss the results based on specifications with intercepts; the without-intercept tests deliver qualitatively similar results. To correct for the estimation errors in the first stage, TableIVreports standard errors based on the Shanken (1992) corrected standard errors. By construction, covariance with the NI-index is tantamount to covariance with innovations to the state variable of consumption growth. Many models featuring a persistent component would predict that an asset that performs poorly when there is a negative shock to long-run consumption growth will command a positive risk premium.13In these models, the coefficient estimates on the NI-index should be positive. In the stand-alone NI-index model, the coefficient estimate of news innovations is positive and significant at the 1% level. Loadings on the NI-index command a positive premium, which is consistent with the theoretical predictions of these models.
The stand-alone NI model explains 62% of the variance in average portfolio returns and has an annualized intercept of 2.47%. For comparison, we document the second-stage regression results for the CAPM, the Fama and French (1993) three-factor model, and the Fama and French (2016) five-factor model. The CAPM explains only 27% of the variance in portfolio returns. The Fama and French (1993) three-factor model and the Fama and French (2016) five-factor model do better with adjustedR2s of 38% and 62%, respectively. The stand-alone NI model outperforms the CAPM and the three-factor model by a large margin and performs comparably to the five-factor model in terms of adjustedR2. The three-factor annualized intercept is 2.54% and the five-factor annualized intercept is 1.70%. We also report the NI-index associated annualized Sharpe ratios. The associated Sharpe ratios from the Fama and MacBeth (1973) regressions have the standard interpretation of Fama and MacBeth (1973) slopes as returns on factor-mimicking portoflios. The associated Sharpe ratios are calculated as the average slope divided by the time-series standard deviation of these slopes. The implied Sharpe ratios are large in magnitudes, ranging between 0.56 and 0.92 across the different specifications. Panel A of Figure2plots the model predicted log expected return against the log average realized return of the 51 test portfolios for the NI-index model. Figure2also plots the model predicted log expected return against the log average realized return of the 51 test portfolios for the Fama and French (1993) three-factor (Panel C) and Fama and French (2016) five-factor (Panel D) models.
We also test whether the NI-index is robust to the inclusion of the Fama-French factors as controls. The pricing power of the existing factors may arise in part from movements in the persistent component of consumption growth. If this is the case, then we would expect the NI-index coefficient estimate to no longer be significant when controlling for the other factors. We note that even if the significance of the NI-index disappears in the presence of other factors, this is still an important finding, since our results would potentially explain one source of the existing factors' pricing power. However, we find that the NI-index remains significant in all specifications. Model 5 of TableIVshows the second-stage regression results for the NI-index and the market excess return. The point estimate of the NI-index coefficient is similar to the stand-alone model and is significant at the 1% level. Model 6 controls for the Fama and French (1993) three factors. Again, the point estimate is similar to that of the stand-alone model and is significant at the 1% level. Model 7 controls for the Fama and French (2016) five factors. The point estimate is reduced to about 50% of its original level but remains significant at the 1% level, suggesting that part of the pricing power of the Investment and Profitability factors may come from capturing innovations to the state variable of consumption growth.
We follow the same methodology to test the asset pricing power of the HNI-index. The coefficient estimate on the HNI-index is positive and significant at the 5% level. The adjustedR2of the one-factor model is 47%, which is higher than the CAPM and Fama and French (1993) three-factor model adjustedR2s. We further test whether the HNI-index is robust to the inclusion of the Fama-French factors as controls. The point estimate on the HNI-index is similar to the stand-alone model with the inclusion of the market excess return or the Fama and French (1993) three-factor model. Controlling for the Fama and French (2016) five factors, the point estimate on the HNI-index is reduced with a decrease of about 31% of its original level. The coefficient estimate on the HNI-index is significant at the 1% level. Panel B of Figure2plots the model-predicted log expected return against the log average realized return of the 51 test portfolios for the HNI-index model.
TableVshows the annualized pricing errors based on specifications with intercepts for the stand-alone NI model, the HNI-index model, and the benchmark models. Panel A of TableVshows the mean absolute pricing errors for each model. The annualized pricing error for the NI model is 1.73%, which is smaller than that of the CAPM (2.16%) and of the Fama and French (1993) three-factor model (1.80%). The HNI-index model has an annualized pricing error of 2.01%. The five-factor model has the lowest mean absolute pricing error at 1.43%. Panel B of TableVgives the detailed breakdown of annualized pricing errors of the NI model, the HNI-index model, and the Fama and French (2016) five-factor model. The absolute pricing errors of the NI-index model (HNI-index model) are smaller than the Fama and French (2016) five-factor model for eight (six) of the 25 Size-BM portfolios, seven (four) of the 10 momentum portfolios, and four (five) of the 10 Industry portfolios. In total, the stand-alone NI-index model (HNI-index model) outperforms the five-factor model for 19 (15) out of the 51 portfolios. In TableIA.VIII, we provide additional test statistics on the pricing errors. Theχ2$\chi ^{2}$-statistics on the pricing errors based on the NI- and the HNI-indices are insignificant at the 5% level, and they are much smaller than those based on the CAPM, three-factor, and five-factor models. In addition, we providet-statistics that test whether the average of the pricing errors of an individual asset is different from zero, where the pricing errors are the difference between the time-series returns of the asset and the predicted expected return. The means of the pricing errors of most of the equity portfolios are insignificant using any of the NI-index model, HNI-index model, or Fama and French (2016) five-factor model. The means of the pricing errors of the bond portfolios are significant using the NI- and the HNI-index models. The means of the pricing errors of the one-year and two-year bond portfolios are significant using the Fama and French (2016) five-factor model.
The stand-alone NI-index and the HNI-index models outperform the CAPM and the three-factor models, and perform comparably to the five-factor model across many dimensions. The prices of risk of the NI- and the HNI-indices are significant and robust to the inclusion of the Fama-French factors. The NI-index and the HNI-index models explain a large amount of the variation in average portfolio excess returns across a set of diverse portfolios. Finally, the pricing errors of the NI-index and the HNI-index models are reasonably small and comparable to those of the Fama and French (2016) five-factor model.
The CAPM and Fama-French factor models provide a useful benchmark for evaluating the asset pricing power of the NI-index and HNI-index models. However, a comparison with economically motivated macroeconomic factors may provide additional insight. In this section, we compare the NI- and the HNI-indices with the ultimate consumption risk factors from Parker and Julliard (2005), which have an intuitive relation with our measures. Parker and Julliard (2005) construct ultimate consumption risk factors as future consumption growth over various horizons. There is a relationship with our factors since the NI- and the HNI-indices are innovations to our measures of the persistent component, which we show predict consumption growth at long horizons.
First, we compare the ultimate consumption risk factors with the NI-index. We implement the Fama and MacBeth (1973) two-pass regression approach to test the cross-sectional asset pricing power of different two-factor models: the NI-index alongside cumulative future consumption growth over different horizons. We report the results in TableIA.IX. The table reports results based on specifications with and without intercepts. We discuss the results based on specifications with intercepts; the without-intercept tests deliver qualitatively similar results. The left-hand columns of TableIA.IXdocument the results based on the NI-index. Consistent with Parker and Julliard (2005), we find that the coefficient estimates on the ultimate consumption risk factors are positive for the factors constructed using future consumption growth at all horizons. For example, with the inclusion of the Parker and Julliard (2005) factor constructed at the five-year horizon, the adjustedR2improves to 66%, from 62% in the baseline NI-index model. The coefficient estimate of the NI-index factor falls slightly to 112.14 compared to 114.97 in the baseline model.
Next, we compare the ultimate consumption risk factors with the HNI-index. The right-hand columns of TableIA.IXdocument the results of similar two-factor models using the HNI-index alongside the ultimate consumption risk factors as in Parker and Julliard (2005). We find that the coefficient estimates on the ultimate consumption risk factors are positive for the factors constructed using future consumption growth at all horizons. For example, with the inclusion of the Parker and Julliard (2005) factor constructed at the five-year horizon, the adjustedR2improves to 51%, from 47% in the baseline HNI-index model.
The pricing power of the NI-index (HNI-index) and the Parker and Julliard (2005) factors appear to be complementary. The coefficient estimate on the NI-index is lower with the inclusion of the Parker and Julliard (2005) factors, but the decrease is smaller in magnitude (2.5% using the five-year horizon future consumption growth) compared with the inclusion of the Fama and French (2016) five factors (45.6% decrease). The coefficient estimate on the NI-index remains significant in all specifications. The differences in the asset pricing power of the NI-index and the Parker and Julliard (2005) factors can arise for several reasons. The NI-index captures the innovations to the persistent component of consumption growth, while future cumulative consumption growth captures the level of the future realized consumption growth. The level of future cumulative consumption growth consists of the level of the persistent component and the transitory shocks to consumption growth, both of which are different from the NI-index. Furthermore, although we show that the N-index strongly predicts future cumulative realized consumption growth, the N-index captures investors' current beliefs on the persistent component of consumption growth, which may be different from the realization (e.g., Hirshleifer, Li, and Yu (2015)). The N-index is important as an ex ante measure and as a real-time indicator of investors' belief of the persistent component of consumption growth.
In a recent paper, Dittmar and Lundblad (2017) show that historical cumulative consumption growth prices equity returns in the cross section. As a further robustness check, we test two-factor models using the NI-index (HNI-index) alongside historical cumulative consumption growth at different horizons. We report the results in TableIA.IX. We find that the pricing power of the indexes is robust to the inclusion of historical cumulative consumption growth. In SectionVI, we also consider a comprehensive list of macroeconomic variables similar to Ludvigson and Ng (2007,2009).
In this section, we form portfolios based on preranking betas on the NI-index. The betas are computed using past five-year rolling window regressions. We form quintile portfolios based on ex ante NI-index betas and show that the resulting portfolios have a large and significant spread in average returns. Our sample includes all stocks that are in the major stock exchanges: NYSE, Amex, and NASDAQ. We exclude firms in the financial industry or firms with SIC code between 6000 and 6999. Using the full sample of traded stocks avoids the criticism that the NI-index only explains the average returns of the portfolios that we study, providing an important diagnostic despite the many challenges associated with the portfolio sorting procedure based on the preranking betas. For each quarter, we sort the stocks based on their estimated betas over the past five years (60 months), which we denote as preranking betas. We compute the betas by regressing monthly firm-level excess returns on the NI-index, controlling for the market excess returns. We require at least 20 nonmissing returns for each stock. We rank stocks based on the estimated preranking betas and form value-weighted portfolios. We perform this exercise each quarter and report the average returns and postranking betas of each group. We also report the average return and postranking beta of the long-short zero-investment portfolio spread.
We present the results in TableVI. Panel A of TableVIshows results based on betas estimated using the NI-index and Panel B shows results based on betas estimated using the HNI-index. Each panel reports the average excess return, standard deviation of return, annualized Sharpe ratio, standard error, and average postranking beta for each of the quintile portfolio as well as the long-short strategy. The average excess returns, the annualized Sharpe ratios, and the postranking betas increase largely monotonically from the first portfolio to the fifth portfolio. The long-short strategies based on both the NI- and the HNI-indices generate positive and statistically significant spreads. The magnitudes of the spreads are about 0.25% per month or 3% per year. The long-short strategy return based on the NI-index is significant at the 10% level and that based on the HNI-index is significant at the 5% level.
We use this portfolio sorting approach based on the ptrranking betas as an alternative and supporting method in this paper because sorting on nontraded factor covariance tends to be a noisy procedure. It is known that sorting on characteristics is less noisy than sorting on covariances, making it difficult for factors formed on covariance sorts to capture the underlying discount factor variation. In other words, even if past covariances are perfectly measured, they may not measure future conditional covariance perfectly. Accordingly, we take the portfolio sorting result that the long-short strategy produces a positive and significant return as strong evidence in support of our main results: loadings on the NI-index (HNI-index) in the cross section of individual stock returns are associated with higher expected returns.
article.V. Second Moments:
In this section, we investigate the extent to which our results are driven by second-moment information embedded in our measures. We focus on two primary channels: (i) the N-index (HN-index) captures information about the stochastic volatility of consumption growth, and (ii) the N-index (HN-index) captures uncertainty about macroeconomic conditions because this uncertainty drives news coverage around topics related to economic growth prospects. Extracting second-moment information contained in our measures is useful for understanding the connection between our predictability and asset pricing results. First, we construct a measure of stochastic volatility using information embedded in our measures and remove this component. We test the extent to which our predictability and asset pricing results are driven by this measure of stochastic volatility. Second, we test whether our predictability and asset pricing results are driven by information about uncertainty contained in existing measures in the literature.
There is a debate in recent literature about the effect of uncertainty shocks on growth (e.g., Bloom et al. (2007), Bansal et al. (2014), Berger, Dew-Becker, and Giglio (2020), and Ludvigson, Ma, and Ng (2021)). We investigate the extent to which our measures capture information about stochastic volatility in consumption growth. We construct a measure of stochastic volatility,VNI$V_{NI}$, as the trailing 60-month variance of the NI-index. In addition, we construct an alternative measure of stochastic volatility,VHNI$V_{HNI}$, as the trailing 60-month variance of the HNI-index. We then test the extent to which our consumption growth predictability and cross-sectional asset pricing results are driven by second-moment information embedded in the N- and the NI-indices.
Following the same procedure, we construct a measure of the persistent component orthogonal to the second-moment information embedded in the HN-index and label itHN(res)$HN(res)$. We find that the predictive power of this measure remains strong with coefficients positive at all horizons and significant at the 5% level from the three- to six-year horizons. The adjustedR2s peak at 28% around the five- to six-year horizons. These findings indicate that the consumption predictability results are driven primarily by information in the N-index about the level of the persistent component of consumption growth.
We explore these results further by testing the asset pricing power of the HNI-index alongside a corresponding measure of innovations to stochastic volatility. Following a similar procedure as described above, we construct a measure of innovations to stochastic volatility and label itΔVHNI$\Delta V_{HNI}$. We present the results on the right side of Panel A in TableVII. In the specification with an intercept, the inclusion of our measure of innovations to stochastic volatility,ΔVHNI$\Delta V_{HNI}$, substantially improves the asset pricing power of the HNI-index. The coefficient estimate onΔVHNI$\Delta V_{HNI}$is significant at the 5% level. The adjustedR2of the two-factor model (HNI-index andΔVHNI$\Delta V_{HNI}$) increases to 61%. This improvement is larger than the 62% to 66% increase we document when addingΔVNI$\Delta V_{NI}$as a pricing factor alongside the NI-index. The coefficient estimate on the HNI-index decreases only slightly from 7.60 to 7.25.
Panel B of TableVIIpresents the 51 test portfolio pricing errors for the two-factor models of the NI-index (HNI-index) alongside theΔVNI$\Delta V_{NI}$factor (ΔVHNI$\Delta V_{HNI}$). The results are based on specifications with intercepts. The inclusion of the volatility measure alongside the HNI-index reduces portfolio pricing errors compared to the pricing errors of the baseline HNI-index one-factor model reported in Panel B of TableV, especially for the momentum portfolios. Overall, these results indicate that a substantial component of the pricing power of the NI-index arises from the low-frequency component of the N-index that is tied to consumption growth predictability.
One potential concern is that the N-index captures time-varying uncertainty about macroeconomic conditions instead of long-run consumption growth prospects. For example, Baker et al. (2016) show that uncertainty is countercyclical—uncertainty increases during economic downturns. It is possible that the N-index captures a countercyclical second moment that drives both the consumption growth predictability and the cross-sectional asset pricing results. In this section, we directly test whether our results are driven by correlation with macroeconomic uncertainty.
In our main consumption growth predictability tests, we show that the inclusion of realized industry volatility does not affect the point estimate of the N-index or the overall adjustedR2at any horizon. In TableIA.XI, we confirm that the other uncertainty measures such as the EPU measure of Baker et al. (2016), the GPR of Caldara and Iacoviello (2022), and RMV have a minor impact on our predictability results. Furthermore, we test whether innovations to these measures affect our cross-sectional asset pricing results. TableVIIIpresents the results of the Fama and MacBeth (1973) regressions of the NI-index (HNI-index) alongside the uncertainty measures. Controlling for the innovations to the uncertainty measures, the magnitudes of the coefficient estimates on the NI-index (HNI-index) are similar to those of the baseline models. The coefficient estimates on the innovations to the uncertainty measures are not statistically significant. Overall, we find that neither the predictability results nor the cross-sectional asset pricing results are driven by time-varying uncertainty around macroeconomic conditions.
We also test whether higher moments drive our results. We employ a measure developed by Manela and Moreira (2016) to examine whether time-varying rare disaster probabilities account for our results. Manela and Moreira (2016) construct an NVIX index that they show contains information about the probability of rare disasters. In TableVIII, we also document the asset pricing results from a two-factor specification that includes innovations to the NVIX as a pricing factor. The inclusion of innovations to the NVIX alongside the NI-index (HNI-index) does not qualitatively change our results.
article.VI. Additional Tests:
We provide an additional robustness test of our cross-sectional asset pricing results in the spirit of Lewellen, Nagel, and Shanken (2010). If the portfolios can be spanned by a few underlying factors, then by chance, even a random stream of noise may be able to explain a large portion of the variation in the test portfolio returns. To address this concern, we simulate 100,000 streams of white noise and test the cross-sectional asset pricing power of these noise series using the same 51 test portfolios over the same period. We construct the noise factor adjustedR2distribution. The results are reported in TableIA.XII. The adjustedR2of the NI-index outperforms the 99thpercentile of the noise factor distribution and the adjustedR2of the HNI-index outperforms the 95thpercentile of the noise factor distribution. These findings suggest that the pricing power of the NI- and the HNI-indices is highly unlikely to occur by chance.
Under the long-run risk framework in Bansal and Yaron (2004), both the dividend-price ratio and the risk-free rate can be expressed as affine functions of the persistent component of consumption growth and stochastic volatility. Inverting this system, Constantinides and Ghosh (2011) discuss how the persistent component can be expressed as an affine function of the dividend-price ratio and the risk-free rate. Based on this discussion, we compare our measures of the persistent component with price-dividend ratio,14pd, and the risk-free rate,rf$r^{f}$. TableIXdocuments results for four different measures: the N-index, the N-index level measure stripped of the stochastic volatility component (N(res)$N(res)$),HN$HN$-index, and the HN-index stripped of the stochastic volatility component (HN(res)$HN(res)$).
The first four columns report the coefficients from a regression of log price-dividend ratio on each measure. Columns (5) to (8) show the coefficients from a regression of the risk-free rate on each measure. For each of the four measures, the coefficient on the risk-free rate regression is positive and significant, which is consistent with the parameterization from Bansal and Yaron (2004) where an increase in expected growth rates increases the risk-free rate. For the N- and the HN-indices, the coefficients from the price-dividend ratio regressions are insignificant and negative. This is not consistent with standard calibrations where the IES is assumed to be greater than unity so that the intertemporal substitution effect dominates the wealth effect. In response to higher expected growth, households buy more assets and the price-dividend ratio also rises. However, the insignificant and negative relationship may be due to the fact that the N- and the HN-indices contain information about stochastic volatility, which distorts the coefficient estimates in these regressions. Rows 2 and 4 of TableIXdocument the regressions using the volatility-stripped versions of the N- and HN-indices, respectively. In both cases, the coefficients on the price-dividend ratio are positive and significant at the 1% level, consistent with the predictions under the standard parameterization as in Bansal and Yaron (2004). Overall, we find the predicted relationship between our measures of the persistent component and the risk-free rate.
Furthermore, we test the implications of Constantinides and Ghosh (2011) by regressingN(res)$N(res)$andHN(res)$HN(res)$on both the price-dividend ratio and the risk-free rate. We report the results in the last two columns of TableIX. The directions of the coefficient estimates are consistent with the model predictions, but the adjustedR2s of the two regressions are low at 0.014 and 0.017. These results suggest that the price-to-dividend ratio and the risk-free rate do not span our measures of the persistent component of consumption growth. This result is consistent with our findings that the N- and the HN-indices strongly predict future cumulative consumption growth and that the predictive power is distinct from macroeconomic variables. One reason that the price-dividend ratio and the risk-free rate do not span the N- and the HN-indices is that the price-dividend ratio and the risk-free rate likely capture information beyond the persistent component of consumption growth.
In SectionII.A.2, we discuss several alternative measure construction choices. First, we construct a version of the N-index using a different pasting choice to combine the Proquest series with the Factiva series. We construct the measure by scaling the Factiva series by the ratio of the two standard deviations (Proquest to Factiva) in the overlapping period as the scaling factor. We refer to this measure as the “alternative-pasting N-index.” Second, we decompose the N-index by separating consumption from output-related words to create two alternative measures: the “consumption-only N-index” using only the word “consumption” and the “output-only N-index” using the output-related words “GDP,” “GNP,” “Gross National Product,” “Gross Domestic Product,” and “Pig Iron.” Finally, we construct a measure using counts of the phrase “Industrial Production” (the “IP N-index”) and a separate measure using the phrase “Payroll Employment” (the “Employment N-index”) as keywords.15
We construct innovations to these measures by fitting each measure to an AR(1) model and extracting the residuals and then test the asset pricing power of these innovation measures. We label these innovation measures in the same fashion (i.e., the alternative-pasting NI-index). We report the results in TableIA.XIII. The first row of Panel A shows the correlations between the N-index and the alternative measures: the Alternative-pasting N-index, the consumption-only N-index, the output-only N-index, the IP N-index, and the employment N-index. The second row of Panel A shows the correlation between the NI-index and the innovation measures of the alternative indices. The alternative-pasting index is highly correlated with the N-index with a correlation of 0.97. Innovations to this measure are highly correlated with the NI-index with a correlation of 0.94. The correlations of the N-index with the consumption-only N-index and the output-only N-index are 0.80 and 0.60, respectively. The correlations of the NI-index with innovations to these measures are both 0.75. The correlations between the N-index and the IP N-index and the employment N-index are low at 0.08 and 0.11, respectively. The correlations of the NI-index with innovations to these measures are also low at 0.10 and 0.28, respectively.
We use these alternative measures in our main consumption growth predictability test and report the results in Panel B of TableIA.XIII. The alternative-pasting N-index performs comparably to the N-index. The coefficients are positive at all horizons and significant from the one- to six-year horizons. The adjustedR2peaks at the six-year horizon at 19% compared to 25% for the baseline measure. The consumption-only measure predicts consumption growth with positive coefficients at all horizons. The coefficients are significant from the one- to eight-year horizons. The adjustedR2s are lower, peaking at 11% at the six-year horizon. The coefficients on the output-only measure are positive at all horizons and significant from the three- to six-year horizons. The adjustedR2peaks at 17% at the 10-year horizon. The industrial production and payroll measures do not predict consumption growth. The coefficients are a mixture of positive and negative and not significant.
We implement the Fama and MacBeth (1973) two-pass regression approach using innovations to the alternative measures as pricing factors and report the results in Panel C of TableIA.XIII. The coefficient estimate on the alternative-pasting NI-index is positive and significant at the 1% level using the Shanken (1992) adjusted standard error. The adjustedR2is 55%, which is higher than that of the Fama and French (1993) three-factor model. The output-only model performs well. The coefficient estimate on the output-only NI-index is positive and significant at the 5% level and the adjustedR2is 51%. The coefficient estimate on the consumption-only NI-index is positive and significant at the 1% level. The adjustedR2is 32%, which is slightly below the adjustedR2of the Fama and French (1993) three-factor model. The IP NI-index and employment NI-index measures perform similarly with adjustedR2s of 30% and 38%, respectively.
Overall, the consumption predictability and asset pricing results are driven by both consumption and output components. Both the output-only and the consumption-only measures significantly predict consumption growth at long horizons. In the cross-sectional asset pricing tests, the output-related words perform well in terms of coefficient estimate and adjustedR2. The consumption-only measure does not perform as well, although the coefficient estimate is still positive and significant at the 1% level.
In the previous section, we examine how macroeconomic uncertainty affects our baseline results. Here, we further study the relationship between our news measures and other major macroeconomic variables. We consider a comprehensive list of macroeconomic factors similar to Ludvigson and Ng (2007,2009), where we group the list of variables into four broad categories: financial ratios, firm-side variables, consumer-side variables, and housing variables. Specifically, we include each macroeconomic variable in the Fama and MacBeth (1973) two-pass regression alongside our indexes and study whether the inclusion of the macroeconomic variables affects the results on the indexes.
We first examine the financial ratios, which include the term spread, credit spread, TED spread, inflation rate, financial sector leverage growth from He et al. (2017), T-bill rate, long-term return, corporate bond return, gold return, and oil return.16We report the asset pricing results in TableIA.XIV. Panel A reports specifications using the NI-index and Panel B reports specifications using the HNI-index. The coefficient estimates on most of the macroeconomic variables are not statistically significant at the 5% level. In contrast, the NI-index (HNI-index) coefficient estimates remain statistically significant at the 5% level for all specifications controlling for the macroeconomic variables and are close to the point estimates in the baseline specifications without controlling for the corresponding time spans.
The second category we examine is the firm-side macroeconomic variables, which include various industrial production measures, dividends, earnings, net issuance, various employment-related measures, and the average work hour measure.17We report the results in TableIA.XV. Panel A reports specifications using the NI-index and Panel B reports specifications using the HNI-index. The index coefficient estimates remain statistically significant at the 5% level for all specifications controlling for the macroeconomic variables. The point estimates on the NI-index (HNI-index) in the specifications controlling for the macroeconomic variables in the second category are close to those in the baseline specifications without controls for the corresponding time spans. The employment growth coefficients tend to be significant and increase the adjustedR2s.
The third category we examine is the consumer-side macroeconomic variables, which include two personal income measures, eight unemployment-related measures, and durable consumption.18We report the results in TableIA.XVI. Panel A reports specifications using the NI-index and Panel B reports specifications using the HNI-index. The coefficient estimates on the indexes remain statistically significant at the 5% level for all specifications controlling for the macroeconomic variables. The coefficient in the specification controlling for unemployment-related variables experiences a relatively larger drop. For example, controlling for the unemployment rate change, the point estimate goes from 114.97 to 97.04 (15.59% decrease) based on the NI-index and from 7.60 to 6.01 (20.92% decrease) based on the HNI-index.
The last category we examine is the housing-related macroeconomic variables, which include five housing start measures and five housing authorized measures.19We report the results in TableIA.XVII. Panel A reports specifications using the NI-index and Panel B reports specifications using the HNI-index. The index coefficient estimates remain statistically significant at the 5% level for all specifications controlling for the macroeconomic variables. The point estimates in the specifications controlling for most of the macroeconomic variables in the fourth category are actually slightly larger than those in the baseline specifications without controlling for the corresponding time spans.
Overall, it is difficult for the traditional macroeconomic variables to capture the innovations in the persistent component. Controlling for the macroeconomic variables has a limited effect on our baseline predictability and asset pricing results. These results suggest that one reason the return-based factor models can better explain the cross-sectional returns relative to the macroeconomic variables is that the factor models can capture some information about the innovations to the persistent component of consumption growth.
Kleibergen and Zhan (2020) raise several concerns about the two-pass regression, which are highly relevant to our paper since this is the approach we implement in our asset pricing tests. The validity of factor risk premia tests depends on the correlations between the risk factor and test asset returns and on the number of time-series observations of the risk factor relative to the number of test assets (referred to by the authors as the “limited T vs. large N problem”). Identification of factor risk premia in the second-pass regression comes from variation in estimated portfolio betas. Kleibergen and Zhan (2020) propose a test statistic to identify situations in which there may be insufficient variation in portfolio betas to identity factor risk premia and then apply this test to several existing alternative consumption measures (Parker and Julliard (2005), Jagannathan and Wang (2007), Savov (2011), Kroencke (2017)). In these settings, this test cannot reject the null that the test portfolio factor betas are constant, which suggests that the Fama and MacBeth (1973)t-test statistic may not be a reliable test of the risk premia of these alternative consumption measures.
These findings suggest that care should be taken when implementing the two-pass regression approach, especially when testing a consumption-based risk factor such as in our study. To address this concern, we implement the test statistic proposed by Kleibergen and Zhan (2020) to test whether the portfolio loadings on the NI-index provide sufficient identification for the factor risk premium in the second-pass regression. Our model has only one risk factor and we implement the two-pass regression without a zero-beta return. Therefore, based on Kleibergen and Zhan (2020), testing for the identification of the risk premium is equivalent to testing the null hypothesis that the test portfolio factor betas are zero. We run the first-pass time-series regressions to estimate the 51 test portfolio betas on our one-factor NI-index model. We then follow the approach documented in Kleibergen and Zhan (2020) and implement an F-test of the null hypothesis that the test portfolio betas equal zero and report thep-value. Thep-value is 0.005, so we reject the null hypothesis that the portfolio betas equal zero at the 1% level.20
This result is consistent with the authors' emphasis on having sufficient time-series data for the portfolio beta estimation. As discussed in Kleibergen and Zhan (2020), the “limited T vs. large N problem” is one of the primary challenges facing existing alternative consumption measures since the number of covariance terms that need to be estimated is too large given the limited time-series observations. An advantage of our news-based measure is that it can be constructed at higher frequencies (monthly), which results in a number of time-series observations that is an order of magnitude larger than for the annual-based consumption measures—in our setting, the “limited T vs. large N problem” is unlikely to be an issue.
In TableIA.XVIII, we report the coefficient estimates with Shanken (1992) adjusted standard errors below each point estimate. From left to right, the first specification is the baseline model using changes in an alternative consumption growth measure,ΔX$\Delta X$, as the single pricing factor. The second specification in the center column is the one-factor model usingΔXres,N$\Delta X_{res,N}$as the pricing factor. The final specification on the right-hand side is the one-factor model usingΔXres,HN$\Delta X_{res,HN}$, the equivalent measure calculated using the HN-index instead of the N-index, as the pricing factor. Overall, we find that after removing the persistent component of consumption growth, as captured by the N-index (HN-index), the pricing power of the alternative consumption growth measure decreases. The magnitudes on the coefficient estimates and the explanatory powers decrease by about 20% across the different specifications, suggesting that about 80% of the pricing power of these five alternative consumption measures comes from the short-horizon component.
article.VII. Conclusion:
In this paper, we document the existence of a persistent component in consumption growth. We take a novel approach using news coverage to capture investor concern about economic growth prospects. We call this news measure the N-index. We find that consumption growth is highly predictable by the N-index, especially over long horizons, which directly supports the existence of a persistent component. The N-index predicts future consumption growth and the predictive power peaks at the 72-month (six-year) horizon with an adjustedR2of about 25%. We show that the predictive regression results are robust to corrections for overlapping samples and persistent regressors and using several alternative measures of consumption growth. We construct an alternative measure of the N-index that removes measurement noise using a one-sided HP filter. We call this measure the HN-index and find a robust pattern of even stronger consumption predictability results.
Next, we take innovations to the N-index and construct another measure called the NI-index. We show that the NI-index has significant cross-sectional asset pricing power using the Fama and MacBeth (1973) regression framework on 51 test portfolios. Loadings on the NI-index are compensated by positive risk premiums. The NI-index outperforms the CAPM and Fama and French (1993) three-factor model and performs comparably with the Fama and French (2016) five-factor model in pricing the testing portfolios. The asset pricing power of the NI-index remains significant with the inclusion of the ultimate consumption risk factors from Parker and Julliard (2005). We find similar results using a measure of innovations to the HN-index. The evidence suggests that a persistent component of consumption growth is an important pricing factor in the cross section of asset prices.
Overall, our findings suggest that news coverage and investor concern about economic growth are driven in part by investor perception of long-horizon economic growth prospects. Our paper provides a narrative approach that links investor perception, persistence in consumption growth, and asset prices.
